quick_facts.label: Role in the criminal-court structure
quick_facts.value: A Judicial Magistrate First Class (JMFC) presides over a Court of Judicial Magistrate established for a district or local area. Under the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (BNSS), the State Government establishes such courts after consulting the High Court, and the High Court appoints their presiding officers.

quick_facts.label: Statutory sentencing ceiling
quick_facts.value: Under BNSS section 23, a Court of Magistrate of the First Class may impose imprisonment up to three years, a fine up to ₹50,000, both, or community service, subject to the court's jurisdiction and the applicable offence provisions.

quick_facts.label: Entry pathway
quick_facts.value: The usual entry route is the relevant State Judicial Service / Civil Judge (Junior Division) competitive recruitment process, often called PCS-J. Requirements, age limits, languages, reservations, examination scheme, and the precise designation are set state by state rather than through one national JMFC examination.

quick_facts.label: Baseline qualification example
quick_facts.value: A law degree is the central educational requirement in state rules and notices. For example, Haryana's rules require an LL.B. from a university established by law and approved/recognised by the Bar Council of India, while the Sikkim Judicial Service notice required a law degree from a university established by law in India.

quick_facts.label: Designation and posting
quick_facts.value: The entry cadre may be labelled Civil Judge (Junior Division), Civil Judge-cum-Judicial Magistrate, or another state-specific equivalent. A judicial-service member can be assigned civil work, judicial-magisterial work, or both, depending on the state and posting.

sections.heading: Core judicial-magisterial responsibilities
paragraphs: A JMFC is a judicial officer in the subordinate criminal judiciary. The officer conducts criminal proceedings allocated to the court, decides matters within the court's territorial and subject-matter jurisdiction, issues judicial orders, and tries offences that the applicable criminal law and its schedules make triable by that court.

The BNSS provides the operational framework: Courts of Judicial Magistrates are constituted in districts; their presiding officers are appointed by the High Court; and the Chief Judicial Magistrate may define local areas within which magistrates exercise their powers. The statutory sentencing power of an JMFC is limited to imprisonment up to three years, fine up to ₹50,000, both, or community service. These are ceilings, not a description of every case an individual court will receive.

A JMFC is distinct from an Executive Magistrate. This node concerns the judicial branch: adjudication in criminal courts under the High Court-led subordinate judiciary, rather than executive or revenue administration.
bullets: Conduct hearings and criminal trials or other proceedings assigned by law and by roster.
Assess evidence and legal submissions, record reasons, and issue reasoned judicial orders.
Exercise only the jurisdiction and powers conferred by statute, notification, and High Court allocation.
Work within the supervisory structure of the Chief Judicial Magistrate and the High Court.

sections.heading: How entry through PCS-J / State Judicial Service works
paragraphs: There is no single all-India JMFC recruitment examination. Article 234 of the Constitution provides the constitutional framework for appointments to a state's judicial service other than district judges: appointments are made by the Governor in accordance with state rules, after consultation with the State Public Service Commission and the High Court. In practice, the responsible recruiting body and process vary by state; a High Court, State Public Service Commission, or state-authorised process may publish the recruitment notice.

Candidates normally target an entry judicial post such as Civil Judge (Junior Division) or Civil Judge-cum-Judicial Magistrate through the state-specific judicial-services examination. An LL.B. is the core qualification, but applicants must treat the live notification and governing state rules as controlling: age range, domicile or local-language requirements, computer knowledge, category relaxations, character/medical requirements, number of vacancies, and examination stages can differ.

For illustration only, Haryana's published rules set age and LL.B./basic-computer requirements for Civil Judge (Junior Division), while a Sikkim Judicial Service notice for Civil Judge-cum-Judicial Magistrate (Grade III) required Indian citizenship, a law degree, an upper-age condition, state-language communication ability, and computer knowledge tested at viva voce. These examples should not be treated as universal eligibility rules.
bullets: Complete a qualifying LL.B. from an institution acceptable under the applicable state rules or notification.
Monitor the relevant High Court and/or State Public Service Commission recruitment portal for a live Civil Judge / Judicial Service notice.
Prepare for the stated selection stages, which commonly include a screening or preliminary examination, written main examination, and viva voce, where prescribed.
After selection, complete appointment formalities, posting, probation, and the state judicial academy's induction programme where applicable.

sections.heading: Capabilities the role demands
paragraphs: The role requires strong command of criminal procedure and the ability to apply statutory limits on jurisdiction, sentencing, and case handling. Because the officer must decide proceedings through judicial orders, disciplined legal analysis, careful evaluation of the record, clear legal writing, and procedural fairness are central professional capabilities.

Digital-court capability is increasingly relevant. Haryana's entry rules expressly include basic computer-application knowledge, and the Madhya Pradesh State Judicial Academy schedules ICT and e-Courts induction programming for newly recruited Civil Judges. Training calendars also include criminal rules and court-rules programmes for serving civil judges, illustrating the continuing professional-development demands of the job.
bullets: Criminal-law and criminal-procedure analysis.
Reasoned judgment and order writing.
Evidence assessment, hearing management, and procedural accuracy.
Impartiality, judicial temperament, confidentiality, and professional ethics.
Working competence with court technology and electronic records.

sections.heading: Where and under whom the officer works
paragraphs: The workplace is ordinarily a district-level subordinate court or a court serving a defined local area. The BNSS contemplates Courts of Judicial Magistrates in every district and permits the establishment of special JMFC courts for particular cases or classes of cases. Exact court location, subject allocation, and local jurisdiction are determined through the statutory and High Court administrative framework.

The High Court appoints the presiding officers of Courts of Judicial Magistrates and may appoint a JMFC as Chief Judicial Magistrate or Additional Chief Judicial Magistrate. At entry, officers may first undergo institutional and field induction training before or alongside independent judicial assignments; for example, a Madhya Pradesh High Court posting order required newly appointed Civil Judges (Junior Division) to undergo both forms of induction training.
bullets: District and subordinate criminal courts, with postings transferable under the relevant state service rules.
Potential assignment to a combined civil-judge and judicial-magistrate role where the state cadre so provides.
Administrative supervision through the Chief Judicial Magistrate structure and the High Court.
Judicial-academy induction and continuing education as prescribed by the state.

sections.heading: Career progression is state-service specific
paragraphs: Progression is governed by the particular state's judicial-service rules and High Court administration; it is not automatic merely from holding an JMFC posting. The BNSS expressly allows the High Court to appoint a JMFC as Chief Judicial Magistrate, Additional Chief Judicial Magistrate, or—in a sub-division—Sub-divisional Judicial Magistrate, subject to the statutory framework and High Court directions.

A common broad pathway in state subordinate judiciaries is movement from an entry Civil Judge (Junior Division)/judicial-magistrate role to senior civil-judge or chief-judicial-magistrate responsibilities and, subject to the relevant rules and selection methods, to the higher judicial service. Haryana's published higher judicial-service rules, for example, identify Civil Judges (Senior Division), Chief Judicial Magistrates, and Additional Civil Judges (Senior Division) as the feeder group for specified promotional routes. Titles, quotas, qualifying service, examinations, and seniority rules differ by state.
bullets: Entry judicial-service posting: Civil Judge (Junior Division), Judicial Magistrate, or a combined equivalent.
Possible higher magisterial responsibilities: Additional Chief Judicial Magistrate, Chief Judicial Magistrate, or Sub-divisional Judicial Magistrate, where appointed by the High Court.
Potential advancement into senior civil-judge and higher judicial-service cadres under state rules.

sections.heading: Compensation context: state pay matrices, not a single national salary
paragraphs: There is no single nationwide JMFC salary. Pay, allowances, probation terms, and revisions are determined by the relevant state government and judicial-service rules, so candidates should rely on the current recruitment advertisement, appointment order, and pay-revision notifications for their state.

As a dated illustration rather than a current universal rate, a Madhya Pradesh High Court posting order dated December 5, 2023 recorded newly appointed Civil Judges (Junior Division), Entry Level, on a temporary basis in the ₹77,840–₹136,520 Level J-1 scale. A Sikkim Judicial Service recruitment notice for Civil Judge-cum-Judicial Magistrate (Grade III) likewise advertised Pay Matrix J-1 at ₹77,840 plus admissible allowances. Actual take-home pay varies with state rules, allowances, deductions, posting, and later pay revisions.
bullets: Check the live state notification for the applicable pay level and allowances.
Do not use historic pay scales or another state's pay matrix as a guaranteed salary estimate.
Account for probationary status, location-linked allowances, and statutory deductions when comparing compensation.
